summary: Iteration 4 — P1(a) EU 561/2006 break rule + Pydantic input validation. Backend: 31/31 pytest PASSED (19 regression from iter3 + 12 new). New tests cover: detect-rest/entries Pydantic 422 on bad date/time formats, compute_break_rule for 5 scenarios (6h/30=violation, 4h/45=ok, [270,270]/45=ok, [270,270]/30=violation, [270,270]/[15,30]=documented), dashboard cycle.break_violations_count, recompute on PUT/DELETE. Frontend (420x900): registered fresh user, EntryForm live indicator shows red 'Règle 4h30 / 45 min non respectée (6h00 sans pause qualifiante)' (breakrule-warning) and green 'Règle 4h30 / 45 min respectée' (breakrule-ok); after submitting a violation entry, ComplianceBar tile 'Pauses 4h30 — 1 alerte' shown via indicator-break-violations; History page shows red 'Pause 4h30/45min' badge with data-testid=break-violation-<id>. Dashboard legacy 'Archive' badge handled in iter3 (no regression).

minor.issue: Spec says driving=[270,270] with rest=[15,30] should be 'ok' (15+30=45 split), but algorithm flags 'violation' (max=540). Reason: algorithm checks break[i] AFTER accumulating drive[i], so during the 2nd 270 drive the accumulator reaches 540 (>270min limit) BEFORE the qualifying 30-min break resets it. The implementation is reasonable (a violation occurred chronologically), but contradicts the test_finish review_request spec. Decide which interpretation is canonical: either update spec text, or change algorithm to defer max-update until next break window is evaluated.

action_items: Decide canonical behavior for [270,270] with [15,30] split case. If 'ok' is required: algorithm must track break-window cumulatively and only flag violation when 4h30 elapses without a qualifying break (45min total with one 30+) WITHIN the running window — currently max_acc is captured greedily before late-arriving 30-min break can reset.
(carry-over from iter3) Consider splitting server.py (~600 lines) into routers/.
critical_code_review_comments: compute_break_rule semantics — see backend.minor[0]. Greedy max tracking conflicts with EU 'split-break' interpretation in spec.
DailyEntryIn.driving_segments / rest_breaks have no per-element bounds (negative or huge ints accepted). Add Field(ge=0, le=24*60) on List items via conlist for hardening.
Pydantic Field(pattern=...) added for date/start_time/end_time on DailyEntryIn and DetectIn — validation now returns 422 cleanly (verified).
recompute_cycle_counters now also writes break_violations_count (verified via update/delete tests).
server.py is ~600 lines; still under 700-line threshold.

success_rate.frontend: 100% on tested flows (live indicators + compliance tile + history badge)

test_credentials: admin@routier-facile.fr / Admin123!. Fresh test user created each run: TEST_brk_*@example.com / Passw0rd!.
seed_data_creation: pytest test_break_rule creates TEST_brk_* user with ~6 entries spanning various break-rule scenarios; playwright created TEST_e2e_brk_* user with 1 violation entry.
context_for_next_testing_agent: All P1(a) features verified end-to-end. Pytest suite at /app/backend/tests/ has 31 cases (test_break_rule.py adds 12 new). Frontend testids to remember: breakrule-ok, breakrule-warning, indicator-break-violations, break-violation-<id>. Open question: spec vs algorithm discrepancy for [270,270]/[15,30] split — currently algorithm flags violation, test documents actual behavior with a print statement (not a hard assertion).
rca of the issue: N/A — no blocking bugs. Single spec/algorithm discrepancy documented in minor issue and action_items.
